linkedin_drafts.title: Guardrails, not prompts
linkedin_drafts.body: Most agent failures are not model failures. They are operating failures: no scope, no acceptance rule, no delivery lock, and no one clear moment where work counts as done. AtlasOps Agent Skill Packs package the missing operating layer: SKILL.md files, AGENTS.md files, checks, blockers, QA contracts, and delivery policies.

linkedin_drafts.title: Codex repo control
linkedin_drafts.body: If your coding agent keeps changing too many files, the fix is not another prompt. It is a repo control guardrail: ownership boundaries, test commands, dirty-worktree rules, and evidence before completion.

linkedin_drafts.title: Stripe delivery verification
linkedin_drafts.body: Checkout redirects do not unlock private delivery. Downloadable products release after payment verification matches the order.

linkedin_drafts.title: Social publishing confirmation
linkedin_drafts.body: Opening a social composer is not a post. Typing text into a composer is not a post. Public content automation needs permalink confirmation, source discipline, and a rule against internal draft labels.

linkedin_drafts.title: RAG source discipline
linkedin_drafts.body: A RAG workflow should not sound confident just because it retrieved something. The useful layer is a retrieval evidence packet: current sources, validation notes, conflict handling, and a clear gate for unsupported claims.

linkedin_drafts.title: OpenClaw + Codex safely
linkedin_drafts.body: Tool bridges need doctor checks, gateway checks, version awareness, rollback plans, and repo boundaries. That is the difference between an integration and a reliable operating path.

linkedin_drafts.title: Agent Company Launch Kit
linkedin_drafts.body: When agents start acting like a team, they need company-shaped controls: owners, tickets, routines, budgets, QA review, and acceptance requirements. Agent speed needs an operating system.

linkedin_drafts.title: Revenue agents
linkedin_drafts.body: Revenue automation should be strict about what counts. Leads are not sales. Checkout sessions are not revenue. Test payments are not live revenue. Signed payment verification is where the state changes.

linkedin_drafts.title: AI SEO guardrails
linkedin_drafts.body: AI visibility work needs more than new content. It needs entity clarity, service-page trust signals, FAQ structure, and checks that prevent unsupported visibility claims.

linkedin_drafts.title: Setup review
linkedin_drafts.body: A Guardrail Setup Review is for the buyer who already knows the workflow is important and wants Atlas to check the install path, evidence rules, and failure modes before agents use it.

x_drafts: Guardrails, not prompts. Agent Skill Packs package checks, blockers, evidence rules, SKILL.md files, AGENTS.md files, and QA contracts for real workflows.
Checkout links and success redirects do not unlock private delivery. Delivery waits for payment verification.
If Codex keeps touching too many files, add repo control: scope, ownership, tests, dirty-worktree rules, and review before done.
Social automation needs permalink confirmation. Browser-open and composer-open are not posts.
RAG agents need retrieval evidence packets: current sources, validation notes, uncertainty, and conflict handling.
OpenClaw + Codex needs doctor checks, gateway status, rollback plans, and repo boundaries before tool actions count.
Agent companies need tickets, owners, routines, budgets, and QA. Heartbeats are not work.
Revenue agents should not count checkout sessions as revenue. Signed payment verification changes state.
Agent Skill Bundles group the controls buyers usually need together: builder, social, revenue, OpenClaw + Codex, and company launch.
The useful question is not 'what prompt should I use?' It is 'what evidence proves this workflow is done?'

website_newsfeed_items.title: Agent Skill Packs are now positioned as operational infrastructure
website_newsfeed_items.summary: AtlasOps now frames Guardrail Store products as reusable controls for agent workflows, not prompt packs.

website_newsfeed_items.title: Bundle paths added for common buyer workflows
website_newsfeed_items.summary: Builder, Social Publisher, Revenue Agent, OpenClaw + Codex, and Agent Company bundles clarify what to buy first.

website_newsfeed_items.title: Ask Atlas product router recommends packs by failure mode
website_newsfeed_items.summary: Buyers can describe a workflow problem and get a pack and bundle recommendation.

website_newsfeed_items.title: Stripe delivery rules stay verification-backed
website_newsfeed_items.summary: Checkout redirects still do not unlock downloads; delivery waits for payment verification.

website_newsfeed_items.title: Social sales drafts remain draft-only
website_newsfeed_items.summary: Drafts were generated for LinkedIn and X, but publishing still requires platform confirmation.

field_note_outlines: Why agent teams need operating controls before more autonomy
Why checkout sessions and payment verification are different
How to turn recurring agent failures into reusable Agent Skill Packs

buyer_objection_responses.objection: Is this just a prompt pack?
buyer_objection_responses.response: No. The packs include operational rules, evidence requirements, agent instruction files, QA contracts, and delivery policies.

buyer_objection_responses.objection: Can I buy one pack instead of a bundle?
buyer_objection_responses.response: Yes. Each $99 Agent Skill Pack is sold separately. Bundles are for workflows that need multiple controls together.

buyer_objection_responses.objection: Does checkout mean I get an instant download?
buyer_objection_responses.response: No. Download delivery unlocks only after Stripe payment verification matches the order.
Yes. The Guardrail Setup Review checks the fit, install path, evidence rules, and acceptance criteria.

buyer_objection_responses.objection: Do you publish social posts automatically?
buyer_objection_responses.response: No. Drafts can be prepared, but publishing requires platform confirmation and approval.
